1. Body without Organs (BwO): This concept is an attempt to describe a plane of potentiality or intensity, a pre-organized state that exists before a structured organism comes into being.

The BwO is a way to rethink the organization of experience, desire, and subjectivity. In this model, individuals are not bound by hierarchical structures or fixed identities, but are instead constituted by the flows and intensities that pass through them.

2. Rhizomes: Deleuze and Guattari use the metaphor of a rhizome, a type of plant that grows horizontally and forms networks, to describe a non-hierarchical, decentralized mode of thinking and organization.

Rhizomes, in contrast to trees, which represent hierarchical and centralized structures, are more flexible and adaptable. They can connect to any other point in a network, and they can grow or shrink as needed.

Had a great discussion yesterday with @qorprate on how psychoanalysis fits in with the thesis of the article below. 

While Schizoanalysis might provide cues on how to live well as digital personae, Lacan and Žižek help diagnose the condition with surprising clarity. https://t.co/QWbt1KmQq5

The split between the bodily/perspectival and the social/narrative dimensions of self maps well onto Lacan's orders: 

The Real vs. the Symbolic and the Imaginary. 

Living as digital dividuals with multiple profiles creates a complicated layering in the Symbolic order.

Profiles are Imaginary selves, projections of how we want to be seen by others. 

Continuous engagement with these digitally mediated selves and therefore reinforcing the rift between the Real might contribute to the schizoid flavour of online interactions.

Žižek clarifies that the unconscious is not concerned with reality/falsity but with pleasure. 

It only knows positivity, so disbelief in online personae adds to the libidinal charge. 

The flatness of digital profiles increases how engaging they are, tantalizingly pure symbols.

It's Kayfabe. Wrestling fans are so engaged with the symbolic/imaginary plots BECAUSE they are fake, not despite it. 

The same goes for social media. The simulation is more interesting.

🧵I recently took an autonomous ride in SF.🚙🤖

I was THRILLED. I kept wondering for months why it was so damn exciting to me. I finally figured it out: 

It was the opposite of Future Shock - I was delightfully tasting the future. I call it TECH RAPTURE. 

Read more below 👇 https://t.co/IL8EVuTgyW

Tech Rapture is the thrill of seeing disruptive tech at work - marked by textures of nerdiness, the joy of being right & societal implications. It’s the giddy elation that powers scientists, entrepreneurs & sci-fi writers. It's the magic of seeing future possibilities materialize

Tech Rapture is for the nerds. 

It’s about understanding how years of research for deep neural nets, image recognition, data pipelines & more, resulted in that autonomous car driving me around. 

The deeper the knowledge, the greater the wonder.

Tech Rapture is also about the joy of being right. 

Many, including me, knew self-driving cars were coming. 

There's an undeniable thrill in being able to say, "I told you so." 

It's the joy of being an early adopter, of riding the waves of innovation.

There's a feeling of participating in history, of shaping how the technological possibilities will manifest. 

With each innovation, we’re carving out a new future. 

And each time, it feels like a chance to witness the future leaking out from the present.

The potential futures are plural. 

Decisions on product, business models, etc., for disruptive technologies can significantly impact society.

 Ethical dilemmas arise with each technological innovation, opening up important and fascinating implications.
For instance, the ethical conundrums around self-driving cars FINALLY vindicate the countless hours philosophers have spent on trolley problems. https://t.co/xZwi91glgV

Just like how stress can either empower or cause anxiety, reactions to technological disruption can vary - from Future Shock to Tech Rapture. 

Note: This is not an apology for naive techno-optimism, more of a description of the phenomenology of being a hyped tech bro.

Dived into e/acc @BasedBeffJezos @bayeslord 

While I agree with many of the specific takes and some of the overall direction, I think e/acc has some fundamental flaws.

e/acc is useful as a response to effective altruism, but we need a synthesis of its antithesis. 

re/acc? 👇

To start, I agree with:

- Growth is good, we need to accelerate tech innovation

- Markets are best at aggregating information & allocating resources, top-down allocation is flawed

- AI dangers are greater if fewer companies control powerful models. Competition is a safeguard

Now to the flaws: 

1) Idealising markets 

We need to apply the systems lens beyond markets. E.g. interaction of the state (framing market), the commons, and culture. 

There are lots of market failures, most importantly that externalities (e.g. environment) are not priced in.

2) ignoring the internal dimension

what is growth good for? a: the flourishing of CONSCIOUS life. 

if we climb the Kardashev gradient but we're all miserable, we lose. 

or worse, we colonise the galaxy but only Van Neumann probes are left (pure replicators @algekalipso).

3) reductionism 

the narrative around applying thermodynamics to economics and social structures is fundamentally flawed. these are different scales of analysis and you can't reduce social phenomena down to physics. 

e/acc also seems to reduce consciousness to intelligence.

to summarise, while e/acc has some good takes, it seems like an apology for traditional liberalism with an attitude of "everything will turn out well, full steam ahead". 

effective altruism is just as flawed, relying on top-down allocation and impossible utilitarian calculus. https://t.co/WKO7wgjUn9

so what could a synthesis look like? 

re/acc could stand for responsible/regenerative acceleration. 

it would combine e/alt's goals of improving well-being long-term with the market-driven approach of e/acc. 

Radical markets (@glenweyl) and a listening society (@HFreinacht).

I keep playing with the trade-off between structure and "going with the flow". Too much structure is self-coercion. Too free-flowy won't commit to anything difficult.  

There is a sweet spot that isn't a compromise but a symbiosis. I can't get to it reliably yet but here are..

..some of the tracks and hunches I've found: 

1) Time is at the center of this. 

Integrating Chronos (structure) and Kairos (flow) by perceiving both at the same time or toggling between them. 

Creating a relationship between past, present, and future selves. Temporal IFS?

2) Getting the trade-off right initially feels like tuning a guitar string - not too rigid, not too loose. 

What feels good is having a core structure (e.g. daily practices) but freedom within that structure. 

Ideally, the structure is fractal, so also weekly, monthly, etc.

3) On a deeper level, there isn't a trade-off at all. Freedom and structure enable each other. 

Structure and discipline build the capacities to be truly sovereign, responsive, and free in the moment. 

Freedom enables choosing (and adapting) the right structures again and again

ok so i went on a @jhanatech retreat last week and not only got to experience some nice feels but also changed some of my outlook on meditation practice 

overall, the retreat was extremely well facilitated and supported, props to the team (the hype is real)

some thoughts below https://t.co/zqH6uZxe0q

i did not think that it was possible to experience jhanas within a week (especially coming from a non-theravada  practice background) 

but i was still craving them, mind you

that mix of doubt + craving was the main obstacle. it happened only once I stopped giving a f (surprise)

what ended up working for me was

1) 15 min  progressive relaxation

2) 15 min. tuning into (any) positive feelings

3) 15 min. "mixing" relaxation and pleasure (usually through the breath)

4) shifting to joy specifically and "spreading" piti through the body

@jhanatech did a great job of boiling down instructions to the essence, basically

enjoy + relax + observe with curiosity (and unfolding each)

no mention of piti and sukha. the progression of jhanas was only introduced at the end

knowing that stuff going in made progress harder

unlocking j1 took me most of the week and the ride from into j3-4 seemed pretty natural/easy from there (didn't get any further, not even sure if 3 or 4) 

while these were extremely pleasurable, they were in the same order of magnitude as other peak experiences (drugs, sex, etc)

much more valuable was the general practice of relaxation and cultivating positive mind states (metta, gratitude, joy, love). 

my relationship with my body has also changed significantly - a week later, i still experience all sensations as pleasurable. 

body as bliss machine

i also learned a ton of mental moves (cc @this_is_silvia). some favs:

1) grounding agitation by locating it in the body and releasing downwards 

2) generate curiosity - "did i know this was gonna happen?" 

3) noticing the shape of attention for detecting distraction faster

yes, jhanas are "just another experience" and i'm still more interested in liberation independent of conditions 

AND raising baseline well-being and cultivating a relaxed, enjoyable state is obviously skillful and conducive to practice (and a good life)

PLEASURE IS GOOD (duh)

while i probably won't practice the jhanas directly anymore for a while, i took a lot from it and already see how it infuses my practice 

thanks for raving about these retreats @nosilverv and @heynibras 

was great to see @m_ashcroft and @tangrenin popping up on zoom as well

p.s. there is a whole rant about views of practice, spiritual materialism, cherrypicking techniques from their practice systems, and commercializing spirituality that i don't want to get into

i CRINGE at the word "spiritual"

why? bc it's a vague pointer used by too large a tent that includes crystal healers, magical thinking, and performative individualism 

i might say smth like "interested in inner experience" instead. why?

short reflection on SEMANTIC DRIFT below

there is a trade-off between LEGIBILITY and SPECIFICITY

everybody understands "spiritual" but because of that, the word is used in so many contexts and loses its meaning at the limit

in addition, a social dimension of using words for coordinating group identity is at play

the limits of the trade-off above are both cringe:

maxxing legibility results in tired clichés and maxxing specificity results in being "that guy" who only talks in neologism

the middle ground between them is where social identity is negotiated through semantics

there is a natural progression (similar to @Meaningness' thoughts on subculture evolution) with any (identity) word:

1) hipsters/geeks come up with it

2) more and more ppl use it as a shibboleth for identity (mops)

3) idiots and sociopaths co-opt it

4) inventing a new word

this process explains SEMANTIC DRIFT, how words change their meaning over time

the legibility/specificity trade-off changes as a word is used increasingly to signal group identity and slowly uses its meaning

that's why I use "metamodern", not "integral" (and will change again)

poasting current ECOLOGY OF PRACTICE 

(causal loop diagram of supporting relationships between practices)

mapping this out is an interesting exercise, some learnings below https://t.co/INLfwgGQNO

first, some practices are keystone practices for the ecology. 

in my case, these are vipashyana meditation and tummo breathwork. it's encouraging to see that those are also my most regular practices (font size) 

some other practices are supporting practices e.g. deity yoga

there are 2 clusters of mind and body-focused practices 

which surfaces another relevant category: 

bridging practices: 

both tummo and yoga have the function of bridging the body and mind cluster 

within a cluster, most practices are mutually supporting

an alternative way of charting this that may add clarity would be to include the "fruits" of each practice e.g. yoga --> flexibility, strength, somatic awareness and then map how these fruits support other practices 

decided not to do that because it would get too messy

another learning is what is not there, e.g. the gaps in my ecology. a few stand out

1) ensoulment (e.g. active imagination, soulmaking): could link well with IFS & deity yoga

2) dyad and group: (e.g. circling, ritual) the only thing there currently is systema (martial art)

finally, it's interesting what I consider as practices vs. not. 

e.g. are writing, cooking, or sex practices? tripping? answering emails?

at the limit, everything becomes practice, which is generally a beneficial frame (esp. as opposed to "everything as tasks")

what are frames? 

- an ontology / map of the world

- a filter or lens; a way of looking

- a set of assumptions/ heuristics

they range from biological to cultural and psychological - it's all frames

frames are adaptive if they lead to effectiveness and well-being https://t.co/HDuL5PmuD0

three recommendations for becoming better at recognising frames: 

- perspective taking (e.g. relationships or novels) 

- becoming aware of cultural assumptions (travel, studying history) 

- vipassana meditation

and practices for breaking frames: 

- thinking outside the box (via concrete technical or creative problems)

- changing your stories (e.g. CBT) 

- straight up demolish frames (through psychedelics or high-dose vipassana) https://t.co/mgJmsSNzWD

key characteristics of ritual according to @DrJohnVervaeke:

systematic transfer and systemic transformation

at the core of how ritual works are inhabiting different perspectives and changing identity 

most importantly, these changes happen on the group level (or at least are witnessed by a group)

standards to evaluate the success of a ritual: 

do I feel connected, do I belong?

"laws rule out, rituals rule in" 

both are necessary for social order

while laws exclude prohibited behaviour, ritual positively suggests templates of how to act 

law works on a propositional level, ritual on a perspectival/participatory level

to create the social binding that ritual aims for, the "credibility enhancing function", ritual uses costly signaling 

there needs to be a COST to ritual 

that's why initiation ritual often involve danger or violence 

or the need for elaborate practice and preparation
